scenario_results.reason: The lane advanced routine broadcast fulfillment only when the traffic log, MAM package, captions, loudness result, automation playlist, as-run log, delivery receipt, and approval note supported closure.
Missing kill date, sponsor flag, rights-window, continuity approval, and media asset evidence produced a blocked exception note instead of speculative loading.
The missing cart, superseded media, failed loudness check, and closing venue window triggered rework and owner routing rather than an unsupported ready state.
The lane refused to mark work complete without as-run, automation, delivery, and master-control evidence.
Third-party clip use and editorial-risk approval were routed to legal, rights, and editorial owners with an evidence packet rather than decided in-lane.
The lane refused unapproved screener and call-sheet delivery and required the secure review portal and approved recipient list.
Missing EAS retransmission, ETRS, station-log, and monitoring-source evidence kept the workflow blocked or escalated to EAS compliance and engineering.
Sponsorship identification and political file sufficiency were rejected as operations-owned decisions and routed to legal/compliance with traffic and script evidence.
The live remote summary separated ready, blocked, and owner states while escalating venue approval and embargoed-copy confidentiality conflicts.
The generic request returned to the orchestrator for station, channel, schedule, traffic, MAM, automation, QC, rights, approval, EAS/public-file, and communication facts.
Tenant policy conflict with public broadcast-source concerns triggered legal/compliance escalation instead of confident synthesis or policy override.
OTT-only adaptation preserved FCC station-rule caveats, applied tenant AS-11/QC/content-security/approval controls, and recorded assumptions instead of overapplying or discarding the public baseline.

notes: All 12 defined scenarios are represented in functionality-map.json scenario_coverage.
All 11 required abilities are mapped and validated.
Functionality coverage is complete for shared, execution-processing-and-fulfillment, and professional-and-information-services requirements.
All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold with complete ability coverage.

The package stays inside broadcast operations and does not claim legal, editorial, FCC compliance, rights, talent-contract, engineering, QC-waiver, contractual, financial, or safety-critical authority.
Runtime strategy remains specification-based until a separately committed retained implementation exists.
